title: Tagliatelle with Spinach, Mascarpone and Parmesan (Not Dairy-Free)
subtitle: A pasta dish for any night of the week.
excerpt: Tagliatelle with Spinach, Mascarpone and Parmesan (Not Dairy-Free). A weeknight pasta — sauce in one pan, pasta in another, finished together.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook. The bones of the recipe sit in the Italian home-cooking tradition documented by writers like Pellegrino Artusi (La Scienza in Cucina, 1891) and Marcella Hazan.

content.text: Tagliatelle with Spinach, Mascarpone and Parmesan (Not Dairy-Free). The dish belongs to the Italian home-cooking register: straightforward, ingredient-led, calm at the hob. Adjustable for what you need.
Pasta in a pan, sauce in another. The trick is the meeting — the noodles finish in the sauce, holding the starchy water that binds the two.

This is a really good dish for kids. Even though they may not eat spinach on its own, I've never had a problem feeding this to my kids. The reason they like it so much is because they can suck on the spaghetti, and the mascarpone and cooking water make a fantastic sauce.

Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil, add the pasta, and cook according to the packet instructions. Meanwhile, get a frying pan or wok warm, add a drizzle of olive oil, the butter, garlic and nutmeg. When the butter melts, add the spinach. After 5 minutes it will have wilted down and will be nice and dark. A lot of the liquid will have cooked away and you'll have wonderful intensely flavoured spinach. At this point season with salt and pepper until it tastes good, then add the cream, mascarpone and a little ladle of cooking water from the pasta. Let this come to a simmer and then season again.

Drain the pasta, reserving some of the cooking water, then stir it into the spinach sauce. Add the Parmesan and toss everything together. Loosen to a nice silky consistency with some of the reserved cooking water, so it doesn't become too claggy. Check once more for seasoning and serve straight away.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong
attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.symptom: Sauce won't cling to the pasta
items.cause: Pasta drained too thoroughly, or sauce too thin.
items.fix: Reserve a ladle of starchy cooking water before draining. Toss it through with the pasta and sauce in the pan over heat — the starch binds everything.

items.cause: Overcooked, or sat in water after draining.
items.fix: Cook a minute less than the packet says, drain, and finish in the sauce. Time it so the pasta meets the sauce in the pan, not the colander.

items.cause: Pan too hot, or cheese added all at once.
items.fix: Lower the heat first. Add the cheese a small handful at a time, stirring continuously, with a splash of pasta water to keep the sauce loose.

items.cause: Tomato or wine hasn't cooked down long enough.
items.fix: Give the sauce another ten to fifteen minutes at a low simmer. A pinch of sugar softens an acidic tomato base.

content.text: Vegetarian: drop the meat and add 200 g mushrooms (chestnut or porcini) sautéed until they squeak.
Lighter sauce: replace cream with crème fraîche stirred in off the heat — same richness, less weight.
The sauce reheats well the next day; cook the pasta fresh.

The dish sits in the Italian home-cooking tradition: a few good ingredients, a long unhurried cook, and a pan that has fed the family for years. Worth keeping its roots in mind when you taste — the silence between flavours is part of the design.
